name: North Korea

colors.symbolism: the revolutionary traditions of the Korean people; the blood shed by patriots in the struggle for liberation
sovereignty, peace, friendship and unity with peoples of the world striving for independence
purity, strength and dignity; a traditional colour of the Korean people

description: A wide central red stripe occupies most of the flag's height, separated from narrower blue stripes at the top and bottom by thin white fimbriations. A white disc charged with a red five-pointed star is set on the red stripe toward the hoist.
history: Adopted on 8 September 1948 with the founding of the Democratic People's Republic of Korea, replacing the Taegukgi (still used in South Korea), which the new regime considered a feudal symbol. The design was approved by Kim Il-sung. The flag is officially called the Ramhongsaek Konghwagukgi ('Blue and Red-coloured Flag of the Republic'). A poem in praise of the flag is part of the national curriculum.

features.symbolism: wide central red stripe symbolises the revolutionary traditions of the Korean people and the strength of the workers and peasants; flanking blue stripes represent sovereignty, peace and the unity of the peoples of the world struggling for independence; thin white fimbriations represent purity, strength and dignity
background for the red star, evoking the traditional Korean Yin-Yang and the purity of the people
the communist revolutionary traditions and the leadership of the Workers' Party of Korea
